title: Light Turns Into Righteousness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: Rooted and Built Up in Him
source_files: Light Turns Into Righteousness - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 1_5-2_2.txt

outline.title: Declaration: God Is Light (1 John 1:5)
subpoints: John proclaims a message he received from Christ Himself, not something he invented
God is light — utterly pure in all His characteristics, without any darkness, sin, or corruption
Light represents both the utter purity of God and the unveiling, purifying effect God has on those who know Him
Darkness is shorthand for all things sinful and for the absence of God's sanctifying presence — there are only two categories in John, never neutral ground
The light of God is ultimately embodied in Jesus Christ: 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12)

outline.title: If Clause 1 and 2: Fellowship Excludes Darkness (1 John 1:6-7)
subpoints: If we say we have fellowship with God yet walk in darkness, we lie — our lives contradict our profession
Fellowship with God means being redeemed by the Spirit, justified, and indwelt by the Spirit — and that produces observable change
Sin and disobedience separate people; obedience to God's Word results in fellowship with one another
Walking in the light is also evidence that the blood of Christ continues to cleanse us — ongoing purification, not just initial justification
Those who live in the truth do the truth — knowing and doing are inseparable in John

outline.title: If Clauses 3 and 4: Confess Your Sins — Do Not Deny Them (1 John 1:8-10)
subpoints: If we say we have no sin (present tense), we deceive ourselves — can you identify specific sins from the last seven days?
Not being able to see specific sin in your life may mean you are not being driven to Christ for forgiveness
If we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness
Confession implies repentance — turning from specific sin, not vague spiritual rhetoric; true repentance will be visible in actions related to the specific sin
If we say we have never sinned (past tense), we make God a liar — He declares all have sinned and fall short of His glory

outline.title: The Conclusion: Do Not Sin — But If You Do, Run to Your Advocate (1 John 2:1-2)
subpoints: John writes so that believers will not sin — Christians must work hard at not sinning, in dependence on the Spirit
Philippians 2:12-13: we work out our salvation because God is working in us to will and to do of His good pleasure
The flip side of Romans 5's grace abounding is not license but deeper amazement and gratitude
If anyone sins, we have an Advocate — Jesus Christ the righteous — standing before the Father interceding for us
He Himself is the propitiation for our sins — His offering is always before the Father covering all our offenses; there is now no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus

summary:
This sermon on 1 John 1:5-2:2 is organized around one central point: those who have seen the light of God through faith in Jesus Christ will live in obedience to Him. Light turns into righteousness. The passage teaches this in two parts — a declaration about who God is (He is light, with no darkness in Him) followed by five conditional statements that apply that truth to how believers must live.

The declaration that God is light (verse 5) establishes the theological foundation for all that follows. John is proclaiming a message received from Christ Himself, not his own invention. God's light represents His utter purity and His purifying, sanctifying effect on those who draw near to Him. Darkness — anything contrary to God's character — is entirely absent from Him. Jesus Christ is the embodiment of that light (John 8:12), and those who follow Him are called to walk in it.

The five if-clauses (verses 6-10) each apply this truth: if we say we have fellowship with God but walk in darkness, we lie; if we walk in the light, we have fellowship with one another and the blood of Jesus keeps cleansing us; if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves; if we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive; if we say we have never sinned, we make God a liar. The teacher emphasizes that the inability to identify specific recent sins may indicate a dangerous self-deception, and that true confession is linked to repentance from specific sins — not vague generalities.

The passage closes with 1 John 2:1-2: John writes so believers will not sin, but when they do, Jesus Christ the righteous is their Advocate before the Father, and His propitiation covers all their sin. The sermon ends with the invitation to not sin, but when you fail, not to try to manage it alone — to run to Christ, who picks you up, sets you on a rock, and gives you a new song.
transcript:
## Introduction: One Point

Though we will consider several verses this morning, I have one single point to communicate: **those who have seen the light of God through faith in Jesus Christ will live in obedience to Him.** That is what I mean by the title, "Light Turns Into Righteousness." The light of God in our lives will turn into obedience on our parts.

This passage teaches this truth in two parts. First, John declares who God is: God is light — perfect in all His characteristics, without any sin, utterly pure. Second, because God is light, that perfection reflects itself in the way we live.

## Part 1: God Is Light (1 John 1:5)

> *"This is the message which we have heard from Him and declare to you, that God is light and in Him is no darkness at all."* (1 John 1:5)

This is the first of many contrasts that John makes in this short letter. For John there is no gray area — everything is this or that. God is light and in Him there is no darkness at all. Light represents two things here: the utter purity of God, and the unveiling and purifying effect God has on those who know Him.

There is nothing that corrupts God — He is completely holy and perfect. And that light shines in the universe and in our lives, driving away whatever darkness is present. Physically, light and darkness cannot coexist — darkness is simply the absence of light. If light is present, darkness cannot be. John makes this doubly clear by saying that in God there is *no darkness at all*.

Darkness in this context means anything contrary to the perfection of God's character — and more: anything that is not *for* God. In John's framework there are only two possibilities: either you are for God or you are against God. There is no neutral ground. Darkness is shorthand for all things sinful, and nothing sinful is present with God.

The light of God is ultimately embodied in the Lord Jesus Christ. In John 8:12, Jesus says:

> *"I am the light of the world. He who follows Me shall not walk in darkness, but have the light of life."*

We often emphasize 1 John 4:8 — "God is love" — and rightly so. But the holiness of God and His unwillingness to put up with sin, taught here in verse 5, is often forgotten. Our God reveals Himself as utterly pure, holy, sinless — and that has profound implications for how we live.

Note also: John is not proclaiming something he invented. He says, *"This is the message which we have heard from Him."* This is what Christ revealed to him and the other apostles. John is not speaking off the top of his head — he is the bearer of the message of Christ.

## Part 2: Therefore, Walk in the Light (1 John 1:6–2:2)

John grabs the truth that God is light and applies it to our lives in a series of five "if" clauses in verses 6 through 10, leading to a final proclamation in chapter 2:1–2 about Christ as our Advocate before the Father.

### Fellowship Excludes Darkness (v. 6)

> *"If we say that we have fellowship with Him, and walk in darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth."* (1 John 1:6)

What does "fellowship with Him" mean? It means: if we say we have been redeemed by the Spirit, that we have believed in the Lord Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of our sins, that we have been declared righteous before the Father, that we have experienced the love of God through Christ, that the Spirit of God dwells in us. If we say all these things are true of us, then certain things will be true in the way we live. If they are not — if our profession of faith has no demonstration in our lives — we are lying.

The Bible assumes that those who have been redeemed by Christ will practice what Scripture teaches. That is not a point up for debate — the Bible simply does not see any other possibility. If you believe the truth of Christ, you will also live it. That is Jesus' own expectation:

> *"Therefore whoever hears these sayings of Mine, and does them, I will liken him to a wise man who built his house on the rock... But everyone who hears these sayings of Mine, and does not do them, will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand."* (Matthew 7:24, 26)

The difference between these two houses is not their structure — it is their foundation. And the foundation here is whether you hear and *do* God's Word.

### Walking in the Light Produces Fellowship (v. 7)

> *"But if we walk in the light as He is in the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus Christ His Son cleanses us from all sin."* (1 John 1:7)

As we live according to God's Word, we have fellowship with one another. Obedience to God's Word results in fellowship with one another — because sin is anti-social. Sin dehumanizes the people in our lives. Paul Tripp writes: *"Because sin is anti-social, it tends to dehumanize the people in our lives. No longer are they objects of our willing affection. Rather, they get reduced to one of two things: vehicles to help us get what we want, or obstacles in the way of what we want."*

Walking in the light is also evidence that the blood of Christ continues to cleanse us. We tend to think of the blood of Christ as what saved us at our first coming to faith. But the blood of Christ is also the means by which God *keeps on* cleansing us. If our lives are not in obedience and harmony with the Word of God, we have no assurance that the blood of Christ is covering our sins.

### Do Not Claim Sinless Perfection (v. 8)

> *"If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us."* (1 John 1:8)

Notice John's ongoing emphasis on the link between obedience and truth. Knowing and doing go together throughout the Scriptures.

If you say you have no sin — that you currently do not sin, that you have achieved some level of moral perfection — you are deceiving yourself. We may not say those exact words out loud. But here is a simple test: look back over the last seven days. Can you identify specific sins that you committed? If you cannot — if you can only say "I'm a sinner" in the abstract, without ever identifying a specific sin — you may be deceived by your own sin.

Why should we be looking at our sin? Because recognizing that you still have sin in your life drives you to Christ. Not recognizing it means you will never be driven to Him. Not being able to say, "I sinned here and here specifically," will keep you from seeing Jesus Christ in His fullness. You need to see your need of Him, even as a believer — so that you can rejoice with Paul: *"O wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death? I thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord!"* (Romans 7:24–25). To rejoice in Christ, you need to see your need of Him.

### Confess Your Sins (v. 9)

> *"If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness."* (1 John 1:9)

Instead of denying our sins, we confess them — we say the same thing about our sin as God says. We acknowledge that we are sinners. We declare specific sins to God. And this implies repentance — turning from specific sins and turning to God.

True repentance will be recognizable in the actions of the repentant sinner. If you are repenting of harsh words you used against someone close to you, true repentance will demonstrate itself in your effort and endeavor to stop using harsh words against them. It will not show itself merely in reading your Bible more — that is good, but it is not linked to the specific sin being repented of. Repentance is always a turning from specific evil.

And the beauty of all this is: God will always forgive His repentant children. Always. This is the sanctifying forgiveness that a child of God experiences when he abandons the sin still present in his life and turns to Christ. True confession rooted in repentance results in holiness — it results in obedience to God.

### If You Deny You Have Ever Sinned (v. 10)

> *"If we say that we have not sinned, we make Him a liar, and His word is not in us."* (1 John 1:10)

Verse 8 addresses those who claim they are not sinning *right now* — present tense. Verse 10 goes further: if you claim you have never sinned, you make God a liar. God says everyone has sinned. To deny the very thing God says about you is to prove His Word is not in you. If all your instincts are to say it is always someone else's fault, that you are always the victim and never the offender, that you have never owned up to anything — the truth of God is not in you. You do not know the God of the Bible.

## Conclusion: We Have an Advocate (2:1–2)

> *"My little children, these things I write to you, so that you may not sin. And if anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. And He Himself is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the whole world."* (1 John 2:1–2)

John addresses his readers as *little children* — a word of tender fatherly affection. He says: I want you to work hard at not sinning. That is the conclusion of everything. Because God is light and there is no darkness in Him, we as children of God will work hard at not sinning.

Christians work hard at not sinning. It is a work of God's grace — but it is a work nonetheless. As Paul says:

> *"Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling; for it is God who works in you both to will and to do for His good pleasure."* (Philippians 2:12–13)

God's amazing grace will never be an excuse for sin or for lack of obedience. But when we do sin — and we will — we are not without hope. Christ stands before the Father interceding for us. He is our propitiation — His offering of Himself is always before God the Father to cover all your offenses. When you sin, you do not try to handle it on your own. You do not hide it or pretend it didn't happen. You turn to God through Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.

Christ has redeemed you so that you can walk according to the light of His Word. That is His will for your life. You have been freed to follow Him. And when you forget that and fall into sin, Christ is right there — He forgives you, sets you back on the way of life, and continues to use you for His glory.
